nodes.label: brAIn
nodes.description: Causal knowledge graph used as Claude's external long-term memory. Stores the extracted structure (atomic claims + typed edges) of documents rather than their text, so future sessions answer 'why X', 'what if Y', 'how A leads to B' without rereading sources. Domain-agnostic; installs in any local folder.

nodes.label: Causal knowledge graph
nodes.description: Graph whose edges are typed by causality (causes, prevents, requires, enables, contradicts) rather than free-form 'links'. The whitelist forces extractors to think in terms of mechanism instead of association — the difference between an indexed search engine and a memory you can interrogate.

nodes.label: Kuzu embedded DB
nodes.description: Single-process embedded property graph database used as the persistent store. Chosen because it embeds in the same Python process, supports Cypher, runs without a server, and is small enough to ship inside the brAIn install. No external dependencies, no admin.

nodes.label: Structure over text storage
nodes.description: brAIn does not archive paragraphs — it stores the extracted causal structure (nodes + typed edges with evidence). Once a doc is ingested, the source can be discarded; the graph keeps what was load-bearing.

nodes.label: On demand retrieval
nodes.description: Claude never loads the whole graph into context. It queries it on demand via brain_find, brain_causes, brain_paths — the graph is long-term memory, the conversation window is working memory.

nodes.label: Causality first principle
nodes.description: When extracting, prefer causal types (causes, prevents, requires, enables) over structural types (part_of, is_a). The audit metric causal_ratio enforces this — extraction is judged on whether it captured mechanism, not just composition.

nodes.label: Source document disposability
nodes.description: Every node description must be self-sufficient. After ingest, the source document must be safely deletable without losing semantic content. This is what distinguishes brAIn from a citation index.

nodes.label: Distinct from textual RAG
nodes.description: brAIn does not store text chunks for similarity retrieval. It stores assertions linked by typed causal edges. RAG returns paragraphs; brAIn returns reasoning chains.

nodes.label: Distinct from note taking system
nodes.description: Linear notes belong in Obsidian or markdown. brAIn captures the disposable substrate of those notes — the causal structure that survives reformulation.

nodes.label: Open node type vocabulary
nodes.description: Node types are a reference vocabulary, not a hard constraint — any non-empty string is accepted. Unknown types are logged to extension_requests.jsonl. Coherence comes from anti-duplicate lookup, not type enforcement.

nodes.label: Strict rel type whitelist
nodes.description: Relation types are strictly enforced. The 12 allowed values are causes, prevents, requires, enables, precedes, contradicts (causal core), is_a, part_of, instance_of, similar_to, property_of (structural), related_to (fallback). Anything else is rejected at ingest. This vocabulary defines what 'traversable' means.

nodes.label: Related to fallback
nodes.description: The unqualified 'related_to' edge type exists as a last resort. audit warns above 5% ratio. Using it is a symptom of skipping the 'why does this edge exist' discipline.

nodes.label: Confidence calibration table
nodes.description: 1.0 = explicit causal verb in the text. 0.7-0.9 = reasonable inference. 0.4-0.6 = plausible hypothesis not demonstrated. Below 0.4 = do not ingest. Audit can flag chains containing low-confidence edges so Claude doesn't smuggle weak inferences into a confident answer.

nodes.label: Single Node single Rel table schema
nodes.description: Kuzu schema has exactly two tables: Node and Rel. Both store the typed value as a property (n.type, r.type) rather than splitting per-type tables. Cypher queries become simpler and the whitelist is a single Python constant — extensibility without DDL changes.

nodes.label: Parallel arrays invariant
nodes.description: On each Rel, sources/evidences/factors are positionally aligned: index i represents one contributing doc_id and its evidence sentence. Length(sources) == length(evidences) == length(factors) MUST hold; audit flags violations as an integrity bug.

nodes.label: Assemble rel arrays helper
nodes.description: _assemble_rel_arrays in lib/ingest.py centralizes parallel-array construction for both create and update branches of _upsert_rels. The doc_id contributes (r.evidence, r.factor); extra source tags (project:brain) contribute empty evidence/factor. Enforces the parallel-arrays invariant by construction.

nodes.label: Edge dedup on triple
nodes.description: An edge is uniquely identified by (src, dst, rel_type). Re-ingesting the same triple from a new doc appends to its sources/evidences arrays rather than creating a duplicate edge. Two different rel_types between the same nodes are kept separate.

nodes.label: Idempotent doc re-ingest
nodes.description: Re-running ingest on a payload with an existing doc_id is safe. The ingester first purges every contribution of that doc_id (removes from sources/evidences arrays, deletes edges whose sources empty out, keeps nodes), then writes the new payload. A document update replaces its previous contributions cleanly.

nodes.label: Tagging convention project prefix
nodes.description: Every node and relation carries two source markers: the doc_id (e.g. project_brain) and the project tag (project:brain). The project tag lets one query filter all nodes of a project regardless of which doc contributed them.

nodes.label: Doc id pattern
nodes.description: doc_id follows project_<name>_<aspect> (e.g. project_brain_lib, project_choros_ui) or project_<name> for unified docs. The first underscore-segment after 'project_' is what derive_project_tag extracts as the project name for auto-tagging.

nodes.label: Project tag auto injection
nodes.description: lib/validate.py derive_project_tag parses the doc_id and injects 'project:<name>' into every node and rel sources before insertion. Removes the previously-manual step where Claude had to remember the convention. Idempotent — already-present tags are not duplicated.

nodes.label: Slugify silent rewrite pitfall
nodes.description: Node id is canonicalized to slugify(label): lowercase, every run of non-alphanumeric becomes a single underscore. Labels with capitals adjacent to capitals (CheckReport -> checkreport), parens, slashes, dots, or numbers attached to letters silently produce ids different from what the extractor wrote. Any rel referencing the pre-rewrite id is skipped at ingest unless caught by the validator first.

nodes.label: Missing endpoint silent skip
nodes.description: Historically, if a rel referenced an id that didn't resolve to any node, the ingester silently dropped that rel. Today fail-loud surfaces this as a check error before ingest, and the CLI exits non-zero — but the data-loss pathology required explicit instrumentation; it was the most common cause of silent graph degradation.

nodes.label: Fail loud design
nodes.description: Every previously-silent failure is now visible: skipped rels print on stderr with file/line, lint warnings list specific labels and chars, brain ingest exit codes 0=clean, 1=lint, 2=real-failure. The CLI refuses error-level payloads unless --force; the MCP path has no --force at all.

nodes.label: Exit code severity
nodes.description: brain.py ingest returns 0 when clean, 1 when only lint warnings (data was written), 2 on real ingest failure (rejected nodes/rels, skipped rels — data loss). The sync agent treats exit<=1 as success because the data did land in the graph; exit==2 triggers the review queue.

nodes.label: Self loop rejection
nodes.description: lib/validate.py rejects any rel where src == dst. A node cannot causally relate to itself in this schema — that's a tautology, not a claim.

nodes.label: Importance clamping
nodes.description: Node importance is coerced to float and clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Missing or unparseable values default to 0.5. Default ensures the audit ordering remains meaningful even on hastily-written payloads.

nodes.label: Confidence clamping
nodes.description: Rel confidence is coerced to float and clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Missing or unparseable values default to 0.8. Below 0.6 the audit flags chains as weak; below 0.4 the extractor is supposed to omit the edge.

nodes.label: Lint issue dataclass
nodes.description: LintIssue in lib/validate.py: kind, target, detail. Surfaces non-blocking quality concerns from the validator (label_forbidden_char, description_too_long, description_too_short, evidence_too_short) so the check command and ingest CLI can list them as warnings.

nodes.label: Description length thresholds
nodes.description: lib/validate.py constants DESCRIPTION_MIN_LEN=30 and DESCRIPTION_MAX_LEN=400. Below 30 the description is too poor to make the source disposable; above 400 it's a paragraph antipattern. Both raise a LintIssue but do not block ingest.

nodes.label: Evidence length threshold
nodes.description: lib/validate.py constant EVIDENCE_MIN_LEN=30. Forces 'X causes Y because Z' explanations on every rel. Single-word evidence ('yes', 'derived', 'related') raises a LintIssue — the threshold catches the laziness that produces unverifiable graphs.

nodes.label: Label forbidden chars lint
nodes.description: Regex LABEL_FORBIDDEN_CHARS in lib/validate.py matches ( ) / . + in labels. When triggered, a LintIssue warns that the id will be silently rewritten by slugify. Pre-emptive — caught at check time so Claude can rename before any rel goes orphan.

nodes.label: Reference node types constant
nodes.description: REFERENCE_NODE_TYPES frozenset in lib/validate.py: concept, entity, event, claim, mechanism, algorithm, property, person, place, artifact, process. Reference vocabulary only — unknown types are accepted but logged to extension_requests.jsonl for human review.

nodes.label: Rel types whitelist constant
nodes.description: REL_TYPES frozenset in lib/validate.py union of CAUSAL_REL_TYPES (causes, prevents, requires, enables, precedes, contradicts), STRUCTURAL_REL_TYPES (is_a, part_of, instance_of, similar_to, property_of), and FALLBACK_REL_TYPES (related_to). Any type outside is rejected at validate_payload time.

nodes.label: Extraction workflow
nodes.description: Seven-step protocol: 1) read full doc, 2) inventory ## sections, 3) first pass extract nodes, 4) second pass extract rels with rich evidence, 5) run brain_check, 6) brain_ingest, 7) gap verification by re-reading source with node list visible. Steps 5-7 turn the discipline into commands rather than mental habits.

nodes.label: Section inventory step
nodes.description: Step 2 of the extraction workflow. List every ##-level heading of the source as a coverage checklist. Each section must produce at least one node or an explicit skip note. Catches the most common extraction failure: skimming late sections.

nodes.label: Node creation filter
nodes.description: Create a node only if the document says enough about the concept to write a real description. A concept merely named in passing does not get a node — wait for a document that actually covers it. Avoids polluting the graph with orphan stubs.

nodes.label: Comprehensive description requirement
nodes.description: Each node description must be self-contained — mechanism, context, key variants, quantitative properties. A reader with only the graph should lose no useful information from this concept. This is the property that makes source documents disposable.

nodes.label: Relation pass evidence quality
nodes.description: Step 4 of the workflow. Each rel must have an evidence field that explains the mechanism: 'X causes Y because Z' is the target. A single vague word is not acceptable; the validator now lints evidence shorter than 30 chars.

nodes.label: Marked decisions capture
nodes.description: Any item the source explicitly marks as decided ([ACQUIRED], decided, validated, frozen) MUST produce a node. These are the most reliable facts in the document — the workflow forbids skipping them.

nodes.label: Rejected alternative extraction
nodes.description: When the source explains why an alternative was not chosen, extract it: create a claim node for the rejected approach, link with contradicts (or prevents) to the chosen approach, full rationale in evidence. The audit's tradeoff_ratio metric measures whether you captured these — without them the graph misses the design 'why'.

nodes.label: Named subcomponent extraction rule
nodes.description: If a parent concept has N named subcomponents, create individual nodes only if each has a distinct cause/effect fingerprint. Otherwise bundle them in the parent's description. Prevents the graph from drowning in low-causal-signal child nodes.

nodes.label: Gap verification
nodes.description: Step 7 of the workflow — cognitively different from extraction: you stop selecting and start comparing. Query all nodes from the doc_id, re-read source section by section, ask 'is this section's core captured?'. Catches semantic omissions structural checks cannot detect.

nodes.label: Anti duplicate check
nodes.description: Before minting a new node, call brain_find to see if an equivalent already exists. When in doubt, reuse — fragmentation is worse than coarseness; brain merge is always available later. The brain_check command formalizes this against the existing graph.

nodes.label: Query graph tool protocol
nodes.description: Agentic-mode anti-duplicate protocol. For each concept the LLM is about to materialize, it calls brain_find on the topic, reads the returned neighborhood, reuses the existing id if semantics match, mints new otherwise. Don't create relations to every neighbor shown — only to what the current document supports.

nodes.label: brain py CLI
nodes.description: brain.py click-based command-line entrypoint. Intentionally kept thin: each command parses args, opens a Kuzu connection via lib.db, dispatches to lib/, prints. Semantic logic lives in lib/; the CLI can be replaced or wrapped (global shim, MCP server) without touching ingestion or audit code.

nodes.label: CLI pure plumbing design
nodes.description: brain.py contains no semantic logic — only argument parsing, connection management, and output formatting. Every command is a thin wrapper over a lib/ function. This separation is what lets MCP, UI backend, sync agent, and CLI share the same engine without duplication.

nodes.label: Click command group
nodes.description: @click.group decorates the top-level cli() function; each subcommand registers via @cli.command(). The --db option is propagated through ctx.obj so subcommands can connect to a non-default Kuzu path (used by the UI backend that points at the live graph from a different cwd).

nodes.label: CLI ingest command
nodes.description: brain ingest <file> runs check_payload first; aborts with exit 2 unless --force. Flags: --force overrides error-level issues, --no-causal-check skips the zero-causal precondition. Auto-prints post-ingest health summary (causal/structural/tradeoff/orphan ratios) so quality is visible immediately.

nodes.label: CLI check command
nodes.description: brain check <file> dry-run validates a payload: schema, slugs, endpoint reachability, causal balance, substring duplicates against the existing graph. Exits 0 clean, 1 with warnings, 2 with errors. Always runs as the first step of ingest; can be called standalone before writing anything.

nodes.label: CLI find command
nodes.description: brain find <pattern> searches nodes by id or label substring (case-insensitive). The lookup-before-create primitive — used by the anti-duplicate workflow and by brain_check's substring duplicate detection.

nodes.label: CLI show command
nodes.description: brain show <node_id> prints a node's full detail: type, description, importance, sources, all outgoing and incoming edges with their type, confidence, and evidence sentences. The atomic 'open this concept' operation.

nodes.label: CLI causes command
nodes.description: brain causes <node_id> --depth N walks upstream via causal-backward edges (causes, prevents, requires, enables, precedes inverted). Answers 'why X happened' / 'what led to X' by traversing the causal subgraph BFS level by level.

nodes.label: CLI effects command
nodes.description: brain effects <node_id> --depth N walks downstream via causal-forward edges. Answers 'what does Y lead to' / 'what does Y enable'. Mirror of causes; uses the CAUSAL_FORWARD subset of the rel whitelist.

nodes.label: CLI paths command
nodes.description: brain paths <src> <dst> --max-hops N finds variable-length paths between two nodes via the Cypher *1..N pattern. Useful for 'how does A relate to B' queries when neither full upstream nor downstream is enough.

nodes.label: CLI context command
nodes.description: brain context <topic> returns the existing graph context around a topic as JSON: substring matches with one-hop neighborhoods. Designed as input to an extraction prompt — pipe it into the system prompt so the model can reuse existing ids.

nodes.label: CLI query command
nodes.description: brain query <cypher> executes a raw Cypher query and prints the result as JSON. Escape hatch for advanced traversal that the higher-level commands don't cover; used heavily during audit.

nodes.label: CLI stats command
nodes.description: brain stats prints node counts by type and rel counts by type, plus totals. The lightweight 'how big is this graph' check, callable from any context with no expensive computation.

nodes.label: CLI audit command
nodes.description: brain audit runs lib/audit.run_audit and prints volumes, health metrics (related_to ratio, no-description ratio, orphan ratio, single-source rels, causal ratio, structural dominance, tradeoff ratio), average confidence per rel type, top-degree nodes, contributions per doc, and warnings/errors. Exit code 0/1/2 reflects severity.

nodes.label: CLI export command
nodes.description: brain export <file> dumps the entire graph (all nodes + rels with their properties) to a JSON file. Used for snapshots, migrations between brAIn instances, and backup.

nodes.label: CLI import command
nodes.description: brain import <file> --strategy {force,merge} loads a JSON dump from brain export. force wipes the DB first; merge cumulates with existing data using the same upsert logic as ingest.

nodes.label: CLI merge command
nodes.description: brain merge SRC into DST collapses two nodes: DST inherits SRC's incoming and outgoing edges and sources; SRC is deleted. Self-loops from the merge are dropped. Irreversible by design — the user is expected to have grep'd both nodes first.

nodes.label: CLI init command
nodes.description: brain init creates the Kuzu DB directory and runs CREATE NODE TABLE / CREATE REL TABLE statements idempotently. Required once per install or after wiping graph/kuzu_db/.

nodes.label: Ingest force flag
nodes.description: --force on brain ingest overrides the pre-ingest check's refusal: even with error-level issues (missing endpoints, zero causal), the ingest proceeds. The MCP brain_ingest tool has no equivalent — overrides are a CLI-only operator decision.

nodes.label: Ingest no causal check flag
nodes.description: --no-causal-check on brain ingest skips the precondition 'payload with >=5 rels must contain >=1 causes/prevents/contradicts edge'. Used by the sync agent because incremental syncs are often purely structural (a new function, a new constant).

nodes.label: lib package
nodes.description: lib/ houses all semantic logic: validate, ingest, check, audit, query, merge, slugify, db, logs, export_import. Both brain.py CLI and mcp_server.py and the UI backend import from lib — single source of truth for graph operations.

nodes.label: lib db module
nodes.description: lib/db.py: DEFAULT_DB_PATH constant, connect(path, read_only=True) returning a kuzu.Connection, init_schema(conn) executing CREATE NODE TABLE / CREATE REL TABLE idempotently, rows(result) helper that materializes a kuzu QueryResult into a list of dicts.

nodes.label: lib validate module
nodes.description: lib/validate.py owns the payload schema: NodePayload and RelPayload dataclasses, ValidationResult with lint_issues, validate_payload normalizing the input, derive_project_tag extracting <name> from doc_id, REL_TYPES whitelist, REFERENCE_NODE_TYPES vocabulary, and the length/char lint constants.

nodes.label: lib ingest module
nodes.description: lib/ingest.py: ingest_payload runs the four-phase pipeline — validate, purge previous contributions of this doc_id, upsert nodes (max-aggregation on importance, first-non-empty description), upsert rels via _assemble_rel_arrays. Returns an IngestReport with creation/update counts, rejected/rewritten/skipped lists, and the project tag injected.

nodes.label: lib check module
nodes.description: lib/check.py owns pre-ingest dry-run validation. check_payload(payload, conn) returns a CheckReport bundling lint_issues, rewritten_ids, rejected_nodes/rels, missing_endpoints, potential_duplicates, causal_check status. has_errors/has_warnings properties drive the CLI's abort logic.

nodes.label: lib audit module
nodes.description: lib/audit.py: run_audit(conn) returns an AuditReport with metrics (volumes, ratios, top-degree nodes, contributions by doc) and warning/error lists. Implements the health thresholds (related_to 5%, orphan 10%, causal 15%, structural 70%, tradeoff 2%). Called automatically post-ingest.

nodes.label: lib query module
nodes.description: lib/query.py: find_nodes substring search, show_node with neighborhoods, causes_of/effects_of BFS level-by-level walks using CAUSAL_FORWARD/CAUSAL_BACKWARD subsets, paths variable-length Cypher, context_for_topic for extraction prompts, run_cypher pass-through, stats aggregation.

nodes.label: lib merge module
nodes.description: lib/merge.py: merge_nodes(conn, src_id, dst_id) re-points all of SRC's incoming and outgoing rels to DST, unions sources, drops self-loops produced by the merge, deletes SRC. Used to absorb duplicate nodes detected post-hoc.

nodes.label: lib slugify module
nodes.description: lib/slugify.py: slugify(label) lowercases, replaces every run of non-[a-z0-9] with '_', strips leading/trailing underscores, truncates at MAX_LEN=80. Raises SlugifyError on empty result. THE canonical id-generation function — every node id ends up running through it.

nodes.label: lib logs module
nodes.description: lib/logs.py: log_extension_request and log_potential_duplicate append JSONL entries to extension_requests.jsonl and potential_duplicates.jsonl. Append-only — readers process forward, writers never rewrite.

nodes.label: lib export import module
nodes.description: lib/export_import.py: write_export(conn, path) dumps all nodes and rels as JSON; read_import(conn, path, strategy) loads back with force or merge semantics. Bundles a version field for schema-evolution safety.

nodes.label: Purge phase
nodes.description: First phase of ingest_payload. For every rel containing the new doc_id in its sources, remove the doc_id from sources and the corresponding entry from evidences/factors. If sources becomes empty, delete the rel. Nodes themselves are never deleted by ingest. This is what makes re-ingestion idempotent.

nodes.label: Upsert nodes phase
nodes.description: Second phase. For each new node: if it doesn't exist, CREATE with this doc_id (and project:brain auto-injected). If it exists, max-aggregate importance, accept the new description only when the previous one is empty (first-non-empty wins), union sources.

nodes.label: Upsert rels phase
nodes.description: Third phase. For each rel: resolve endpoints (try raw id, fall back to slugify), skip with stderr warning if either endpoint absent, dedup on (src,dst,type), assemble parallel arrays via _assemble_rel_arrays, MERGE the edge. Skipped rels are recorded in IngestReport.skipped_rels for the caller to surface.

nodes.label: Max aggregation on update
nodes.description: When a node already exists, the new importance is max(existing, incoming). Captures the fact that any doc claiming this is pivotal raises the floor — a node can never become less important by being re-mentioned in a small context.

nodes.label: First non empty description wins
nodes.description: When a node already exists with a description, ingesting a new doc with a description for the same node keeps the existing one. Only fills the field if it was empty. Avoids descriptions degrading as low-detail docs overwrite high-detail ones — explicit upgrade goes through merge or re-ingestion of the source doc.

nodes.label: Substring duplicate lookup
nodes.description: During upsert_nodes, before creating a new node, the ingester runs a Cypher CONTAINS query against existing labels both ways. Up to DUPLICATE_LOOKUP_LIMIT=5 candidates are logged to potential_duplicates.jsonl with the new id and label. The CLI surfaces the count in the ingest report.

nodes.label: Potential duplicates jsonl
nodes.description: /tmp/brain_test/potential_duplicates.jsonl is the append-only log of substring matches found at upsert_nodes time. Each line: ts, new_id, new_label, candidates list. brain merge consumes this list when a human reviews the dupes.

nodes.label: Extension requests jsonl
nodes.description: extension_requests.jsonl is the append-only log of node types and rejected payload items. node_type_extension entries record any type outside REFERENCE_NODE_TYPES that was nevertheless accepted; rejected entries record schema violations (missing label, unslugifiable, type not in whitelist for rels).

nodes.label: Check payload function
nodes.description: lib/check.check_payload(payload, conn) is the single entry point for pre-ingest validation. Calls validate_payload, then _check_endpoints (each rel.src/dst must exist in payload or graph), _check_causal_balance (>=5 rels and 0 causal triggers refusal), _check_substring_duplicates against the existing graph. Returns a CheckReport whose has_errors property drives the CLI's exit.

nodes.label: Check report dataclass
nodes.description: CheckReport in lib/check.py holds doc_id, project_tag, lint_issues, rewritten_ids, rejected_nodes/rels, missing_endpoints, potential_duplicates, causal_check_passed, causal_check_reason, node_count, rel_count. has_errors true when rejected/missing/causal-fail; has_warnings true when lint/rewritten/duplicates.

nodes.label: Causal required types constant
nodes.description: frozenset(causes, prevents, contradicts) in lib/check.py — strict subset for the pre-ingest causal precondition. Narrower than the audit's CAUSAL_REL_TYPES (which adds enables) because enables alone is too weak a signal that the extractor captured genuine causality.

nodes.label: Causal check minimum rels
nodes.description: lib/check.py constant CAUSAL_CHECK_MIN_RELS=5. Below this threshold the causal balance precondition is skipped — small payloads can be purely structural (a single new component); the rule only bites once the payload claims enough connections to plausibly include causality.

nodes.label: Pre ingest check runs automatically
nodes.description: brain.py ingest invokes check_payload before any write. If has_errors and not --force, the CLI exits 2 with the failing details on stderr. This converts the previously-optional 'brain check first' habit into an enforced step — no way to ingest without check seeing the payload.

nodes.label: Run audit function
nodes.description: lib/audit.run_audit(conn) computes volumes, related_to ratio, causal/structural/tradeoff ratios, description coverage, orphan count and sample, single-source rel count, top-degree nodes (in/out), average confidence per rel type, contributions per doc, parallel-array integrity check. Each computation appends warnings or errors to the AuditReport.

nodes.label: Audit report dataclass
nodes.description: AuditReport in lib/audit.py holds metrics dict, warnings list, errors list. Property exit_code returns 2 if any error (integrity bug), 1 if any warning (threshold breach), 0 clean. Used by brain.py audit's sys.exit.

nodes.label: Causal ratio metric
nodes.description: audit metric (causes + prevents + enables + contradicts) / total_rels. Threshold CAUSAL_RATIO_MIN=0.15: below 15% the graph is too structural and a warning fires. Measures whether the extractor actually captured mechanism rather than mere composition.

nodes.label: Structural dominance metric
nodes.description: audit metric (part_of + requires) / total_rels. Threshold STRUCTURAL_DOMINANCE_MAX=0.70: above 70% the graph is arborescent rather than causal. Triggered alongside causal_ratio failures — together they diagnose the 'pure tree extraction' antipattern.

nodes.label: Tradeoff ratio metric
nodes.description: audit metric contradicts / total_rels. Threshold TRADEOFF_RATIO_MIN=0.02 once the graph has >=20 rels: below 2% the graph captures no rejected alternatives and looks suspiciously decision-free. Forces extractors to surface design tradeoffs as contradicts edges with full rationale.

nodes.label: Related to ratio metric
nodes.description: audit metric related_to count / total_rels. Threshold 5%: above this the extractor leaned on the lazy fallback edge type instead of qualifying causation. Historically the canary metric — every old extraction had to be re-audited when this was added.

nodes.label: Orphan ratio metric
nodes.description: audit metric (nodes with no rels) / total_nodes. Threshold 10%. Orphans are dead weight — every node is supposed to be reachable from at least one other in the graph. Sample of up to 20 orphans is included in the report for inspection.

nodes.label: Post ingest health summary
nodes.description: After brain ingest finishes its writes, _print_post_ingest_audit runs lib/audit.run_audit and prints a 5-line summary: total nodes/rels, then 'causal X% structural Y% tradeoff Z% orphans W% related_to V%'. Quality issues become visible at the same moment the data lands — no separate audit step required.

nodes.label: MCP server py
nodes.description: mcp_server.py exposes 10 brain tools to Claude via the Model Context Protocol stdio transport. Registered globally in ~/.claude/settings.json under 'brain' so every Claude Code session has graph access without per-project setup. Reuses lib/ functions verbatim — the MCP layer is just protocol adapter plus output formatters.

nodes.label: MCP ten tools
nodes.description: The 10 MCP tools exposed by mcp_server.py: brain_find, brain_show, brain_causes, brain_effects, brain_paths, brain_stats, brain_query, brain_ingest, brain_check, brain_audit. brain_check and brain_audit were added when the strict MCP path replaced the lenient one — they let the agent self-validate without shelling out.

nodes.label: MCP global registration
nodes.description: ~/.claude/settings.json declares the brain MCP server with command <BRAIN_REPO>/mcp_server.py. Registration is global, not per-project, so any cwd has access. install.sh wires this on first install.

nodes.label: MCP stdio protocol
nodes.description: mcp_server.py uses mcp.server.stdio.stdio_server for transport — no network port, no HTTP server, zero attack surface. Security boundary is the filesystem; the MCP client (Claude Code) spawns the process and pipes JSON-RPC on stdin/stdout.

nodes.label: MCP read only default
nodes.description: Default _conn() opens kuzu read_only=True. Only brain_ingest reopens with read_only=False. Means a stuck or crashed read tool never holds a write lock that would block subsequent ingests.

nodes.label: MCP one connection per call
nodes.description: Each MCP tool dispatcher calls _conn() afresh rather than holding a long-lived connection. Sidesteps Kuzu's single-writer model and lets multiple tool calls in a single Claude turn proceed without serializing.

nodes.label: MCP strict no force
nodes.description: The MCP brain_ingest tool has NO --force equivalent. If pre-ingest check returns has_errors, the tool returns a refusal message listing the specific issues; the only way forward is to fix the payload. Forces in-session iteration rather than bypassing the validator.

nodes.label: MCP output format mirrors CLI
nodes.description: _fmt_find, _fmt_show, _fmt_chain, _fmt_paths, _fmt_stats, _fmt_check, _fmt_audit in mcp_server.py reproduce brain.py's textual conventions. A user reading MCP output sees the same shape as the CLI — single mental model across both interfaces.

nodes.label: Claude hooks integration
nodes.description: brAIn ships three Claude Code hooks: PostToolUse (brain_hook.sh) tracks Edit/Write, SessionStart (brain_session_start.sh) captures the git baseline, Stop (brain_stop_check.py) launches the background sync agent. Wired in ~/.claude/settings.json with absolute paths.

nodes.label: Post tool use hook
nodes.description: brain_hook.sh: bash script that reads the PostToolUse JSON payload from stdin, extracts tool_input.file_path, appends to /tmp/brain_session_modified.txt. Aborts silently when BRAIN_HOOK_DISABLED=1 (set by sync agent to prevent recursion).

nodes.label: Session start hook
nodes.description: brain_session_start.sh: bash script writing UTC ISO timestamp to /tmp/brain_session_start.txt, clearing leftover session_modified/stop_blocked files, capturing git HEAD of the brAIn repo to /tmp/brain_session_git_head.txt, and surfacing the sync agent's last status and any pending review queue into the SessionStart additionalContext.

nodes.label: Stop hook
nodes.description: brain_stop_check.py: Python script that, when Claude's turn ends, spawns brain_sync_agent.sh in the background via subprocess.Popen with nohup-equivalent detachment (start_new_session, close_fds, DEVNULL streams) and returns immediately with exit 0. The foreground session never waits.

nodes.label: Session modified queue file
nodes.description: /tmp/brain_session_modified.txt: append-only list of file paths edited by Edit/Write tools during the session. Used as a fallback signal for the sync agent when git diff isn't usable; the agent drains the file at the end of each successful sync.

nodes.label: Session start timestamp file
nodes.description: /tmp/brain_session_start.txt: single-line UTC ISO timestamp written by brain_session_start.sh. Historical role was to let the old Stop check compare Node.updated_at against this baseline; now superseded by the git HEAD baseline.

nodes.label: Session git HEAD baseline
nodes.description: /tmp/brain_session_git_head.txt holds the brAIn repo's git HEAD at SessionStart time. The sync agent uses 'git diff $SESSION_HEAD' as a first-sync fallback when no /tmp/brain_sync_baseline snapshot exists yet. Catches edits made by any tool, including Bash cp/mv/sed and external editors.

nodes.label: BRAIN HOOK DISABLED env guard
nodes.description: Environment variable set by brain_sync_agent.sh before invoking claude --print. When BRAIN_HOOK_DISABLED=1, both brain_hook.sh and brain_stop_check.py exit immediately. Prevents infinite recursion: the inner Claude's edits would otherwise queue files and spawn another sync agent.

nodes.label: brain sync agent script
nodes.description: brain_sync_agent.sh: bash worker spawned by the Stop hook. Builds a diff bundle from the snapshot baseline (or git HEAD or queue fallback), runs headless claude --print to extract structural changes into a JSON payload between <<<BRAIN_PAYLOAD>>> markers, calls brain.py ingest --no-causal-check, refreshes the snapshot on success, writes status to /tmp/brain_sync_status.txt.

nodes.label: Background graph sync architecture
nodes.description: The two-session pattern: a foreground Claude does work, a background Claude (claude --print) updates the graph between turns. Eliminates the failure mode where the foreground session ignores the 'update the graph' nag — the graph maintenance has its own process whose only job it is.

nodes.label: Synchronous Stop nag approach
nodes.description: The previous design: Stop hook would block the user's turn-end and ask Claude 'have you updated the graph?'. Reliably ignored on long sessions because (a) text reminders get buffered out, (b) Claude rationally postpones to avoid fragmenting the graph mid-task. Replaced by the background sync agent.

nodes.label: Foreground graph maintenance
nodes.description: The original design assumed the working session itself would maintain the graph. In practice the working session has different cognition (problem-solving) than the archival pass needs (comparison and selection). Demanding both in one head produced systematic skipping of the archival step.

nodes.label: Stop hook background sync shift
nodes.description: On 2026-05-20 brain_stop_check.py was rewritten from a blocking-nag consistency check to a fire-and-forget launcher: spawns brain_sync_agent.sh detached and exits 0. The user observed that even Claude itself was procrastinating the graph update, proving the inline approach wasn't viable.

nodes.label: Incremental snapshot architecture
nodes.description: After each successful sync, brain_sync_agent.sh mirrors every relevant file under /tmp/brain_sync_baseline/. The next sync compares the current working tree to this snapshot file-by-file. Diff bundle shrinks from session-cumulative (~90 KB) to per-turn-delta (~2 KB) — 96% reduction in tokens sent to the inner Claude.

nodes.label: Full redo every sync
nodes.description: Initial sync agent used 'git diff $SESSION_HEAD' every time, which re-sent every change since session start. With re-ingestion idempotent the graph stayed correct, but each sync paid for the cumulative diff. The incremental snapshot replaced this with per-turn deltas.

nodes.label: Snapshot baseline directory
nodes.description: /tmp/brain_sync_baseline/ mirrors a filtered view of the brAIn working tree. Created and refreshed atomically (cp to .tmp dir + mv) so a partial copy never corrupts the baseline. Files larger than 512 KB are skipped to keep the snapshot under ~1 MB total.

nodes.label: Refresh snapshot function
nodes.description: refresh_snapshot() in brain_sync_agent.sh: rebuilds /tmp/brain_sync_baseline/ from scratch using sync_relevant_files. Called only on SUMMARY (ingest success) or NO_CHANGES — failure paths leave the old snapshot intact so the next sync retries the same diff scope.

nodes.label: Snapshot atomic rename pattern
nodes.description: refresh_snapshot copies files into $SNAPSHOT_DIR.tmp.$$, then 'rm -rf $SNAPSHOT_DIR; mv $tmp $SNAPSHOT_DIR'. Even if the agent is killed mid-copy, the next sync sees either the old complete snapshot or the new complete snapshot, never a partial one.

nodes.label: Sync exclude extensions
nodes.description: SYNC_EXCLUDE_EXT regex in brain_sync_agent.sh matches binary and heavyweight file extensions (png, jpg, pdf, zip, whl, so, pyc, fonts, audio, video, sqlite, kuzu). These never carry structural meaning and would inflate the diff bundle without value, so they're filtered before snapshot and diff.

nodes.label: Sync max file bytes
nodes.description: SYNC_MAX_BYTES=524288 in brain_sync_agent.sh: 512 KB ceiling. Files larger are treated as out-of-scope. Catches generated artifacts (screenshots, lockfiles, large datasets) that would either bloat the snapshot or get filtered inconsistently between snapshot creation and diff comparison.

nodes.label: Sync relevant files function
nodes.description: sync_relevant_files() in brain_sync_agent.sh yields the canonical list of files to track: git ls-files plus git ls-files --others --exclude-standard, filtered through SYNC_EXCLUDE_REGEX, SYNC_EXCLUDE_EXT, and the SYNC_MAX_BYTES cap. Called by both refresh_snapshot and the diff-build loop so they're guaranteed to see the same set.

nodes.label: Diff source hierarchy
nodes.description: brain_sync_agent.sh tries three diff sources in order: (a) incremental snapshot — preferred, fast, per-turn delta. (b) git diff vs SESSION_HEAD — first-sync fallback before snapshot exists. (c) PostToolUse modification queue — last resort for non-git repos. First one to find changes is used; others ignored.

nodes.label: Sync agent inline slug rule
nodes.description: Section of the prompt embedded in brain_sync_agent.sh that explains the slugify rule with concrete pitfall examples (CheckReport, 512KB). Necessary because the inner Claude has fresh context and cannot rely on CLAUDE.md being loaded — repeat the trap inline.

nodes.label: Sync agent skips causal check
nodes.description: brain_sync_agent.sh passes --no-causal-check to brain.py ingest. Sync syncs are by nature incremental (add one function, add one filter) and often purely structural; demanding a causes edge on every micro-update would block legitimate ingests.

nodes.label: Single instance lock
nodes.description: brain_sync_agent.sh uses flock on /tmp/brain_sync.lock so concurrent Stop hooks don't spawn duplicate agents. Second invocation logs 'sync already running, skipping' and exits 0 — the first agent will pick up the latest state at its next iteration.

nodes.label: Headless claude print invocation
nodes.description: brain_sync_agent.sh runs 'claude --print' with the diff bundle on stdin. Claude --print is the non-interactive mode of the Claude Code CLI — emits text to stdout, has access to the same MCP servers as interactive Claude, runs under the same auth. 5-minute timeout via /usr/bin/timeout.

nodes.label: Payload markers protocol
nodes.description: The inner Claude is instructed to output its JSON payload between <<<BRAIN_PAYLOAD>>> and <<<END_BRAIN_PAYLOAD>>> markers, followed by a single status line (SUMMARY: N nodes / M rels added, NO_CHANGES, or NEEDS_REVIEW: reason). The bash agent uses awk to extract the JSON without parsing Claude's free-form preamble.

nodes.label: Sync status context surfacing
nodes.description: brain_session_start.sh appends /tmp/brain_sync_status.txt and /tmp/brain_sync_review.txt to the SessionStart additionalContext. The next session opens knowing what the background agent did between sessions; review items persist until the user explicitly removes the review file.

nodes.label: Sync review queue
nodes.description: /tmp/brain_sync_review.txt: written when the agent's payload was refused by brain ingest (exit 2). Contains the Claude output, the ingest stderr, and the payload that failed — enough for a human to diagnose and either rerun the sync or correct the graph by hand.

nodes.label: LMQL constrain at generation
nodes.description: Proposal evaluated then rejected. LMQL constrains LLM output via logit masking at generation time — would prevent payload errors by construction. Cannot be used here: LMQL supports OpenAI and HuggingFace but not Anthropic (no logit access), and the project is dormant since 2023. Anthropic tool-use JSON schemas cover the same role through a different mechanism.

nodes.label: Anthropic tool use strict schemas
nodes.description: The Anthropic-native equivalent of LMQL: input JSONSchemas on MCP tools constrain what the model can emit. brain_check rejects bad shapes pre-write; brain_ingest's strict mode means even error-level payloads cannot be force-overridden. Constraint at the API surface rather than at generation.

nodes.label: Single post tool use queue only
nodes.description: Initial sync agent design relied entirely on /tmp/brain_session_modified.txt — files queued by the PostToolUse hook. Discarded because the queue misses anything not edited via Edit/Write: Bash cp/mv/sed, git commits, external editors all bypass the hook. Replaced by the snapshot-first diff source hierarchy.

nodes.label: UI backend FastAPI
nodes.description: ui/backend/main.py: FastAPI app exposing /api/graph (full graph), /api/node/{id}, /api/search, /api/stats, /api/audit, /api/types, /api/ingest (POST starts an ingestion job), /api/ingest/{id}/status. Backend is a thin wrapper over lib/ — it opens its own Kuzu connection and reuses the same query and ingest functions.

nodes.label: Endpoint ingest post
nodes.description: POST /api/ingest accepts text or a file, builds an ingest prompt template, spawns a Claude session as a subprocess to extract the payload, then runs brain.py ingest. Returns a job_id immediately; the client polls /api/ingest/{id}/status for completion.

nodes.label: Endpoint graph
nodes.description: GET /api/graph returns all nodes and edges (including description, sources, evidences) as JSON for client-side rendering. The whole-graph dump approach trades bandwidth for query simplicity — the React Flow canvas filters locally on importance and search.

nodes.label: Endpoint search
nodes.description: GET /api/search?q=&type=&limit= filters nodes via Cypher CONTAINS on label and description. Distinct from /api/graph because it returns a SUBSET — used by the live search box that auto-expands matched nodes into view.

nodes.label: Claude session subprocess
nodes.description: The UI backend's ingest endpoint launches a child claude process with the ingest prompt template and the user-provided document. The subprocess captures output, parses the JSON payload, and feeds it to brain.py ingest. Background-threaded so the HTTP request returns immediately.

nodes.label: Threading background ingest
nodes.description: ingest endpoint spawns the Claude subprocess in a background thread, returning a job_id over HTTP within milliseconds. Status polling reflects in-memory job state — sufficient because the UI is a single-user dev tool, not a multi-tenant service.

nodes.label: In memory jobs dict
nodes.description: ui/backend/main.py keeps the ingest job registry in a module-level dict {job_id: {status, log, error}}. Lost on restart by design — the underlying ingest is idempotent, so the user can simply re-trigger after a crash.

nodes.label: UI start script
nodes.description: ui/start.sh launches both the FastAPI backend (uvicorn on :8000) and the Vite dev server (frontend on :5173) concurrently, with the Vite proxy forwarding /api/* to :8000. Used during development; production would build the frontend and let FastAPI serve the static assets.

nodes.label: UI frontend React Vite
nodes.description: ui/frontend is a Vite + React 18 SPA. Main components: App (selection state, polling, ingest status), GraphCanvas (React Flow + d3-force layout), NodePanel (node detail view), IngestPanel (modal for the ingest workflow). No global store — props + useState/useMemo.

nodes.label: Graph canvas React Flow
nodes.description: ui/frontend/src/components/GraphCanvas.jsx: React Flow viewport with a custom 'brain' node type rendering a colored circle sized by importance, a label, and a +N badge for hidden neighbors. Edges show rel type as label, arrowhead, dark stroke. MiniMap colored by node type.

nodes.label: D3 force layout
nodes.description: GraphCanvas.jsx runs d3-force simulation (link, charge, center, collide) for 400 ticks synchronously when graphData changes. Computes node positions, which are persisted across expansions via positionsRef so existing nodes don't jump when new nodes appear.

nodes.label: Brain node custom component
nodes.description: BrainNode in GraphCanvas.jsx: renders a colored circle (color from TYPE_COLORS palette, size from importance), a truncated label below (16 chars + ellipsis), and an optional +N badge when hidden neighbors exist. Four invisible handles let edges enter from any side without forcing directional ports.

nodes.label: Importance threshold visibility
nodes.description: App.jsx maintains an importance slider that controls which nodes are rendered. Combined with the expandedNodeIds set (double-click to surface neighbors) it gives the user 'start zoomed out, drill into the dense regions' navigation rather than dumping all 400 nodes at once.

nodes.label: Hidden neighbor badge
nodes.description: When a node has neighbors below the importance threshold, BrainNode shows a +N badge with the hidden count. Double-clicking expands the neighbors into view (adds them to expandedNodeIds). Standard 'progressive disclosure' pattern adapted to a force-directed graph.

nodes.label: Type color palette
nodes.description: TYPE_COLORS in GraphCanvas.jsx: concept blue, entity green, algorithm purple, artifact orange, mechanism red, property pink, event grey, claim amber, person cyan, place lime, process emerald. Unknown types fall back to grey. Same palette drives the MiniMap and the side-panel type filter.

nodes.label: Node panel detail view
nodes.description: ui/frontend/src/components/NodePanel.jsx: side panel showing the selected node's full description, sources, and incoming/outgoing rels with their type, confidence, and evidence sentences. Click an adjacent node to navigate (the canvas auto-expands and centers it).

nodes.label: Ingest panel modal
nodes.description: ui/frontend/src/components/IngestPanel.jsx: modal launched from the App header. Takes text or pasted content, calls POST /api/ingest, polls /api/ingest/{id}/status every 2 seconds, displays the streaming log. On completion, the graph reloads and the new doc's nodes are auto-expanded.

nodes.label: AI wiki corpus experiment
nodes.description: experiments/ai_wiki/ — a 70-Wikipedia-article corpus on ML concepts (neural network, backpropagation, transformer, etc.) used to compare extraction strategies. Cycles include 'no preflight' (naive extraction) and 'with preflight' (LLM consulted graph before each extraction), measuring effects on related_to ratio, orphan ratio, and node consolidation.

nodes.label: Preflight consolidates nodes
nodes.description: Across the AI wiki experiment, runs where the LLM consulted brain_find before minting nodes produced fewer near-duplicate nodes (gradient_descent vs gradient_descent_algorithm vs stochastic_gradient_descent_method). The convergence isn't perfect but the duplicate count dropped substantially.

nodes.label: Preflight cost premium
nodes.description: Preflight cycles cost more API tokens per document — each extracted concept costs an extra query_graph call. The tradeoff is paid once at ingest time and recovered every time the graph is queried (less merging, cleaner traversals).

nodes.label: Heuristic preflight replaced by tool calls
nodes.description: Initial preflight scripts pre-skimmed the source document for likely topics, then queried brain_find on each. This was replaced by giving the extracting Claude direct access to the query_graph tool — the agentic version decides what to query and when, instead of a fixed pre-pass.

nodes.label: Examples sample json
nodes.description: examples/sample.json: reference 18-node, 26-rel extraction describing the causal anatomy of how open-source projects die. Recommended reading before extracting one's first document — shows the granularity, tone, and rel-type coverage expected.

nodes.label: Redis postmortem worked example
nodes.description: Example in docs/SKILL.md: a five-node extraction from a one-paragraph Redis cache misconfig postmortem. Shows the (claim, property, event, property) typing on a real causal chain TTL -> miss storm -> db fallback -> p99 latency, with confidence calibration.

nodes.label: Pytest test suite
nodes.description: tests/ directory: test_audit, test_cli, test_db, test_ingest, test_validate, test_merge, test_query, test_export_import, test_slugify. pytest-driven; runs against a temp Kuzu DB per test. Conftest fixtures provision the schema and clean state between tests.

nodes.label: Global brain shim
nodes.description: ~/.local/bin/brain: optional bash wrapper that execs the brAIn venv's python on brain.py from any cwd. Lets the user type 'brain stats' anywhere instead of 'cd ~/Documents/brAIn && .venv/bin/python brain.py stats'. Documented as the recommended install path.

nodes.label: Brain shim stale path incident
nodes.description: On 2026-05-20 the ~/.local/bin/brain shim was found pointing to a stale .venv path from a discarded test install — breaking every CLI call from outside the brAIn directory. Fixed by rewriting the shim with absolute paths to the current install root. Revealed that a brain doctor command (install-health check) would be a useful addition.

nodes.label: v2 to live migration event
nodes.description: On 2026-05-20 the in-development brAIn-v2 was migrated into the live brAIn by copying 7 files (lib/audit.py, lib/check.py, lib/ingest.py, lib/validate.py, brain.py, mcp_server.py, docs/SKILL.md) over the existing repo. v2 was archived rather than deleted, keeping a rollback option.

nodes.label: SKILL md trim event
nodes.description: On 2026-05-20 docs/SKILL.md was trimmed from 418 to 233 lines (44% reduction) by deleting sections whose rules are now enforced in code: tagging, slug pitfall, anti-patterns, query_graph protocol, re-ingestion semantics.

nodes.label: Parallel arrays bug fix event
nodes.description: On 2026-05-20 lib/ingest.py was fixed: the previous _upsert_rels appended one evidence even when multiple sources were added, breaking the size invariant. Replaced with _assemble_rel_arrays. Bug was latent until the auto-tag injection exposed it.

nodes.label: Sync agent first e2e test event
nodes.description: On 2026-05-20 brain_sync_agent.sh was tested end-to-end on its own deployment diff. Validated the parallel-session architecture and surfaced two design bugs (exit-1 false positive, binary file inflation) that were fixed in the same session.

nodes.label: Incremental snapshot added event
nodes.description: On 2026-05-20 brain_sync_agent.sh gained the /tmp/brain_sync_baseline incremental snapshot mechanism after the user pointed out the full-redo-every-sync inefficiency. Diff bundle dropped from ~90 KB to ~2 KB on small changes; idle re-runs (no changes) take 68 ms with zero API call instead of 2 minutes and a full Claude invocation.

rels.evidence: brAIn IS a causal knowledge graph — the concept and the artifact are functionally identical; the artifact node names the installation, the concept node names the category.
Every brAIn install ships with Kuzu as the storage layer; removing Kuzu means removing brAIn — no other backend is supported.
Storing structure rather than text is the foundational design choice that defines brAIn — it's what separates this graph from an Obsidian vault or a RAG index.
Because brAIn extracts structure rather than archiving paragraphs, the source can be discarded once ingested — the graph carries everything that was semantically load-bearing.
Causality-first is the orientation that distinguishes brAIn from generic graph stores — extractions are judged by their causal density.
Querying on demand requires fast lookups; Kuzu's indexed property graph model supports the substring/neighborhood queries that on-demand retrieval depends on.
Distinguishing brAIn from RAG is a corollary of the structure-over-text storage principle — RAG retrieves paragraphs, brAIn retrieves typed claims.
Same principle applied to a different boundary: linear notes are text-first, brAIn is structure-first.
Without a strict rel vocabulary the graph would degrade into arbitrary 'links' that no causal walk could trust; the whitelist is what makes brain_causes and brain_effects semantically meaningful.
Open node typing is a deliberate asymmetry — rels are strict because traversal depends on them, nodes are lax because their types are categorization hints rather than load-bearing semantics.

The two policies pull in opposite directions on the same surface (validator strictness). Resolved by deciding that rel types determine traversal (must be strict) while node types determine reading (can be lax).
related_to is whitelisted but flagged — it exists as a documented escape hatch with a hard ceiling (5% of edges) so the strict vocabulary doesn't push extractors to invent rel types.
The confidence rubric is a Step-4 input — extractors apply it when setting per-rel confidence; the audit and chain-walking commands consume it later.
The two-table schema is the concrete Kuzu DDL choice — one CREATE NODE TABLE and one CREATE REL TABLE, type stored as a column rather than separate tables per type.

Because all rel types share the Rel table, multi-source provenance must be modeled inside one rel — the parallel-arrays representation is what lets a single edge carry contributions from N docs without splitting.

Centralizing array assembly in one helper means both the create-new and update-existing branches construct arrays the same way — the invariant cannot be silently violated by divergent code paths anymore.
The bug fix on 2026-05-20 was the event that introduced _assemble_rel_arrays — before that the two branches built arrays differently and the invariant was violated whenever a rel had extra sources.
Because edges are keyed by (src, dst, type), re-ingesting the same edge appends to sources instead of duplicating — the dedup is what makes the doc-id purge-then-rewrite cycle safe.
The project: prefix convention is how brAIn tells nodes from different domains apart inside a single graph — it's a foundational part of the multi-project design.
The project_<name>_<aspect> doc_id pattern is what derive_project_tag parses; without the conventional shape the auto-injection couldn't infer the project tag.
Auto-injection derives the tag by regex-matching the doc_id; a doc_id that doesn't follow the pattern produces no tag, and the convention isn't enforced.
derive_project_tag and the sources-list mutation happen inside validate_payload — the validator is where the auto-injection runs before the ingester sees the data.

Before auto-injection, extractors had to remember to add 'project:<name>' manually; in practice the convention was applied to 19% of nodes. Automating it makes the convention universally honored without effort.
When a label slugifies differently from the id the extractor wrote, rels pointing to the original id resolve to nothing — the rewrite is the upstream cause of the silent rel skip.
The lint catches forbidden characters in labels BEFORE the slug rewrite happens, so the extractor can rename and avoid the missing-endpoint cascade. Pre-emptive rather than reactive.
Promoting skipped-rel warnings to stderr with explicit src/dst/type, plus exit code 2, is the operational instantiation of the fail-loud principle — what was silent is now noisy.
The 0/1/2 exit code distinction is the machine-readable channel of fail-loud — sync agent and CI can branch on severity without parsing stdout.
Self-loop check lives in _validate_rel — rejected at parse time so the ingester never sees a tautological edge.
Clamp implemented in _validate_node; ensures downstream ordering operations behave on hastily-written payloads.
Clamp implemented in _validate_rel; combined with calibration table guidance, ensures confidence remains a usable signal.
LintIssue is defined in lib/validate.py and populated by the node/rel validators; ValidationResult.lint_issues carries them out to the ingester and the check command.
Both MIN and MAX constants live in lib/validate.py and are consumed by _validate_node when emitting lint warnings.
EVIDENCE_MIN_LEN declared in lib/validate.py and applied in _validate_rel to flag too-short evidence.
LABEL_FORBIDDEN_CHARS regex lives in lib/validate.py and the lint emission happens inside _validate_node.
REFERENCE_NODE_TYPES is a frozenset in lib/validate.py used by _validate_node to flag unknown types as extension requests.
REL_TYPES is the strict whitelist in lib/validate.py; any type outside it is rejected at _validate_rel.
The constant is the runtime instantiation of the design principle — change the constant and the principle changes too.
The seven-step extraction workflow is the protocol that turns a document into a brAIn payload — it's how brAIn is used.
Step 2 of the seven-step workflow.
Applied during Step 3 (first pass — nodes); decides which concepts get a node vs which stay in a description.
Source-document disposability is only true if every description is self-sufficient; the requirement is the precondition that makes disposability honest.
Step 4 of the workflow — the rel pass with rich evidence.
Applied during Step 3 — anchored facts that must never be skipped.
Applied during Step 3/4 — extracting the 'why not X' is where contradicts edges come from.
The tradeoff_ratio metric only has signal when extractors actually capture rejected alternatives; the rule is what populates the contradicts edges the metric counts.
Applied during Step 3 — decides granularity for parent/child concepts.
Step 7 — the post-ingest comparison pass.
Anti-dup is woven through Steps 3-5 — brain_find before mint, brain_check before ingest.
The agentic protocol IS the anti-dup check when the extractor is an LLM with tool use — it operationalizes 'check before create' as a per-concept tool call.
brain.py is the primary entrypoint to brAIn — it's how every command-line operation reaches the graph.
Every brain.py command imports from lib/; without lib the CLI has no semantic operations.
The plumbing-only nature of brain.py is its defining property — keeping it free of logic enables multiple interfaces (MCP, UI, sync agent) over the same engine.
click is the framework brain.py is built on; the @click.group + @cli.command decoration pattern is the file's spine.
ingest is the primary write command exposed by brain.py.
check is the dry-run validator command added when the strict workflow took shape.
find is the lookup primitive — substring on label/id.
show is the per-node detail command.
causes is the upstream walk command.
effects is the downstream walk command.
paths finds variable-length connectors between two nodes.
context is the prompt-input builder for extraction pipelines.
query is the raw-Cypher escape hatch.
stats is the lightweight volume check.
audit runs the full health computation and prints volumes + metrics + warnings.
export is the snapshot/backup command.
import loads a JSON dump from export.
merge collapses two nodes into one.
init is the bootstrap subcommand — creates the Kuzu node and rel tables in an empty database directory.
--force is a flag on the ingest subcommand.
--no-causal-check is a flag on the ingest subcommand.
ingest's first action is to invoke check_payload; the automatic pre-check IS part of the command's contract.
After a successful ingest, the command always runs _print_post_ingest_audit before exiting — the summary is a byproduct of every ingest invocation.
lib/ houses the semantic core of brAIn — the engine over which every interface runs.
lib/db.py is one of the modules under lib/.
lib/validate.py is one of the modules under lib/.
lib/ingest.py is one of the modules under lib/.
lib/check.py is the most recent module under lib/, added when brain_check became a first-class step.
lib/audit.py is one of the modules under lib/.
lib/query.py is one of the modules under lib/.
lib/merge.py is one of the modules under lib/.
lib/slugify.py is one of the modules under lib/.
lib/logs.py is one of the modules under lib/.
lib/export_import.py is one of the modules under lib/.
ingest_payload uses lib.db.rows for result materialization and the Kuzu connection abstraction.
check_payload calls validate_payload first; check is built on top of validate.
run_audit runs Cypher queries via the lib.db connection helpers.
All query functions use lib.db.rows to materialize results.
Validator calls slugify on every label to compute the canonical id and detect rewrites.
_purge_doc_contributions is the first phase of ingest_payload.
_upsert_nodes is the second phase of ingest_payload.
_upsert_rels is the third phase of ingest_payload.
The purge phase is what makes re-ingestion idempotent — without it, re-running would create duplicate provenance entries.
The max aggregation runs inside _upsert_nodes when the node already exists.
The description-keep policy runs inside _upsert_nodes.
The substring lookup runs for every new node creation inside _upsert_nodes.
The lookup's findings are persisted to potential_duplicates.jsonl.
The helper is called by both branches of _upsert_rels (new edge, existing edge).
check_payload is the main exported function of lib/check.py.
CheckReport is defined in lib/check.py.
The strict subset frozenset lives in lib/check.py.
The threshold constant CAUSAL_CHECK_MIN_RELS lives in lib/check.py.
Both invocations (ingest auto-check and standalone check) run the same check_payload — one as a gate, the other as a preview.
run_audit is the main exported function of lib/audit.py.
AuditReport is defined in lib/audit.py.
_causal_balance computes causal_ratio and appends a warning if below threshold.
_causal_balance also computes structural_dominance and appends its warning.
_causal_balance includes the contradicts/total tradeoff ratio.
_related_to_ratio is one of the metric routines called by run_audit.
_orphan_nodes computes the ratio and samples up to 20 orphan nodes.
The summary calls run_audit and prints a 5-line abstract of its metrics.
Auto-printing health makes invisible degradation immediately visible — a concrete application of fail-loud beyond exit codes.
The MCP server is one of brAIn's three interfaces (CLI, MCP, UI).
mcp_server.py imports lib.db, lib.ingest, lib.query, lib.audit, lib.check — every tool delegates to the lib engine.
The TOOLS list defines all 10 tools; the server registers each via @server.list_tools().
Registering the server globally makes the 10 tools session-wide regardless of cwd.
main() awaits mcp.server.stdio.stdio_server — stdio is the transport.
_conn() defaults read_only=True; only brain_ingest opens with read_only=False.
_dispatch builds a fresh connection at each tool invocation.
brain_ingest's _dispatch branch refuses payloads with has_errors and has no --force code path.
The CLI's --force escape hatch is intentionally absent from MCP — different interfaces, different trust models: a human at a terminal can bypass with intent, a model in a tool loop cannot.
The MCP _fmt_* helpers reproduce the CLI's text formatting so users have one mental model for output.
The three hooks (PostToolUse, SessionStart, Stop) are how brAIn integrates into the Claude Code session lifecycle.
brain_hook.sh is the PostToolUse hook.
brain_session_start.sh is the SessionStart hook.
brain_stop_check.py is the Stop hook.
Every Edit/Write invocation appends the file path to /tmp/brain_session_modified.txt.
brain_session_start.sh writes the UTC timestamp to /tmp/brain_session_start.txt at every session open.
brain_session_start.sh captures git HEAD to /tmp/brain_session_git_head.txt; this is the baseline the sync agent diffs against on the first sync of a session.
The SessionStart hook surfaces the previous session's sync status and review queue into Claude's additionalContext — without this surfacing the background agent's output would be invisible.
brain_stop_check.py spawns brain_sync_agent.sh detached via subprocess.Popen on every Stop event.
The 2026-05-20 event was the rewrite of brain_stop_check.py from blocking nag to background spawn.
The shift is what unlocked the parallel-session design — before it the Stop hook held the foreground.
Without the env guard, the inner Claude's edits would re-fire the hooks and trigger a nested sync agent — recursion. The guard is the precondition for the architecture to be safe.
When BRAIN_HOOK_DISABLED=1 is set, brain_hook.sh exits immediately at the top of the script — no logging, no JSON emission.
The Stop hook short-circuits when the env var is set, so an inner Claude session's Stop event doesn't spawn another sync agent.
The sync agent is the keystone of brAIn's self-maintenance — it makes the graph auto-update from project changes.
brain_sync_agent.sh is the concrete process that implements the background-sync architecture.

The nag-and-block approach and the background-async approach are mutually exclusive ways to wire graph maintenance — one was abandoned in favor of the other because the user observed Claude (including the assistant building it) systematically procrastinated under the nag model.
Maintaining the graph from inside the working session vs from a separate process is the foundational design choice; only one can be the production model.
The snapshot mechanism is implemented as part of brain_sync_agent.sh — refresh_snapshot, sync_relevant_files, the snapshot-diff loop.
The redo-everything-from-SESSION_HEAD approach was replaced by the snapshot-and-delta approach — both can't be the diff source at the same time.
The 2026-05-20 event was when the snapshot mechanism was added to brain_sync_agent.sh.
/tmp/brain_sync_baseline IS the physical baseline — the mechanism is defined by what's in this directory.
refresh_snapshot rebuilds /tmp/brain_sync_baseline at the end of every successful sync.
The tmp-dir + mv pattern is the implementation detail inside refresh_snapshot.
The atomic rename prevents the baseline from being in a partial state mid-update — even if the agent is killed mid-copy, the old complete snapshot is preserved.
sync_relevant_files is defined inside brain_sync_agent.sh as the file-list source for both snapshot and diff.
The extension regex is applied inside sync_relevant_files via grep -vE.
The size cap is enforced inside the per-file loop of sync_relevant_files.
The three-source fallback (snapshot, git HEAD, queue) is implemented in the diff-build section of brain_sync_agent.sh.
Relying on the queue alone vs. layering three sources are alternative architectures — the queue-only approach was dropped after the cp/mv blind spot was demonstrated.

Repeating the slug rule with explicit examples inside the prompt reduces the rate at which the inner Claude produces id/label mismatches — the trap is closer in context than the SKILL.md reference would be.
The agent's --no-causal-check pass is the runtime instantiation of the flag — every sync ingest uses it.
flock on /tmp/brain_sync.lock at the top of the script enforces single-instance execution.
The claude --print call is the core action of the sync agent — the rest of the script just builds context and processes output.
The marker contract is what lets the bash agent extract JSON from the inner Claude's free-form output — without it stdout would be unparseable.
The surface mechanism is implemented in brain_session_start.sh.
Without the agent writing /tmp/brain_sync_status.txt, the surfacing has nothing to read.
When ingest fails (exit 2), the agent dumps the full output to /tmp/brain_sync_review.txt.

LMQL's logit-masking constraint and Anthropic's tool-use input-schema constraint are alternative ways to prevent malformed extraction output — the project couldn't use LMQL because Anthropic doesn't expose logits, so the schema route was taken.
Strict input schemas on MCP tools are what let brain_ingest refuse malformed payloads without a --force escape — the schema rejection happens before any custom validation runs.
The UI backend is brAIn's third interface, alongside the CLI and the MCP server.
The backend's endpoints call into lib.query, lib.ingest, lib.audit directly.
POST /api/ingest is one of the FastAPI route handlers.
GET /api/graph is the bulk-data endpoint feeding the frontend canvas.
GET /api/search is the filtered-lookup endpoint.
Both return node lists, but /api/graph returns everything for client-side filtering while /api/search returns a pre-filtered subset for the search box.
The ingest endpoint spawns a Claude subprocess to perform the extraction.
The endpoint returns immediately and the heavy lifting runs in a worker thread.
The job dict is how the polling endpoint sees the progress of the background thread.
start.sh launches the backend; without it the UI dev setup needs two terminals.
The React frontend is the graphical interface to brAIn.
The frontend depends entirely on the FastAPI backend — every render starts with a /api/graph fetch.
GraphCanvas.jsx is the main rendering component of the frontend.
The d3-force simulation runs inside GraphCanvas before handing positions to React Flow.
BrainNode is registered as React Flow's 'brain' nodeType.
BrainNode colors its circle by looking up TYPE_COLORS — the palette is its color contract.
The importance slider lives in the App component; it's a core UX mechanism.
The +N badge is rendered inside BrainNode based on the data.hiddenNeighbors prop.
NodePanel is the side-panel component invoked when a node is selected.
IngestPanel is the modal component for triggering ingest from the UI.
The AI wiki experiment is brAIn applied to itself as a research subject — measuring extraction quality on a known corpus.
The consolidation finding is one of the observations from the AI wiki cycles.
The cost premium is one of the observations from the AI wiki cycles.
The AI wiki experiment is what revealed the limitations of heuristic preflight and motivated the move to agentic tool-driven extraction.
The tool-driven protocol IS the post-experiment replacement for heuristic preflight.
The sample.json is the canonical reference example shipped in examples/ — pedagogical part of the install.
Both are pedagogical examples — sample.json is JSON form, the Redis postmortem is the inline SKILL.md example.
tests/ ships with the install and is the regression safety net for lib/.
The shim is what lets the user invoke brain.py from any cwd as if it were a globally installed binary.
The incident exposed that hardcoding the venv path at install time is fragile — any move of the install root breaks the shim silently. Motivates a 'brain doctor' command.
The migration is what brought B.1-B.12 into the live codebase — without it the changes would have remained sandboxed.
Auto-injection went live with the migration.
The check module went live with the migration.
Fail-loud became the active default at migration time.
The causal balance metrics were introduced in v2 and went live with the migration.
The trim is a structural change to the brAIn install — SKILL.md is part of the user-facing surface.
The trim was only safe because the rules being removed are now enforced in code — without fail-loud and lint validators, deleting the rules would leave gaps.
The first e2e test is what validated the agent and surfaced the two design bugs (exit-1 false positive, binary file inflation) that became part of the production version.

The auto-tag injection exposed the latent parallel-arrays bug — when the project tag was added as an extra source, the existing code appended only one evidence entry per ingest, breaking the size invariant.
log_extension_request appends to extension_requests.jsonl.
log_potential_duplicate appends to potential_duplicates.jsonl.
